text:
From the manifesto:

“For decades, technology has required standardized solutions to complex human problems. In order to scale software, you had to build for the average user, sanding away the edge cases. In many ways, this is why our digital world has come to resemble the sterile, deadening architecture that Alexander spent his career pushing back against.

This is where AI provides a missing puzzle piece. Software can now respond fluidly to the context and particularity of each human—at scale. One-size-fits-all is no longer a technological or economic necessity. “

This is, if I understand it correctly, in keeping with the position that others have stated that AI tools can enable people to create the small, personal, moldable systems they require without being trapped in one size fits all software produced by megacorps. Those who believe this argument in turn consider this a potentially emancipatory technology. The piece you linked says that this is all nonsense because LLMs can't do that. I'm pointing out that some of signatories have lived experiences that cause them to disagree with that assertion.

text:
a technologist (like myself) building personalised software for myself is very different to building software that other people can mould on the fly. the former was always possible, now it's possible at even faster speed. the latter remains hard: not because of technological problems. "JUST DO WHAT I MEAN" is not a technological problem.

i'm not here to convince you though! i do find it funny how defensive people get about LLMs though

text: Again, I do not personally believe these claims will work out the way they think they will. But I am trying to understand their beliefs and treat them as human beings worthy of my compassion rather than throwing rhetorical stones at them for violating in-group norms

<https://programmingsimplicity.substack.com/p/fundamentals-of-compilers|Fundamentals of Compilers>

text:
> Software can now respond fluidly to the context and particularity of each human—at scale.
A lot hinges on this sentence. I think the sentence is dubious, even without the "at scale". I don't think the _lived experiences_ in question fulfill the first half in ways that non-AI tools fail to.

I think AI is making easy things easier and hard things harder. "I vibe coded my own workout app" is something a person would say, but I think all the people actually saying that know enough about coding that they could also have just coded their own workout app. And nobody is using AI to free themselves from Apple's walled garden or fix Notion's buggy editor.

text:
<@U013ZLJARC7> Thinking about your 80-85% agreement... AI is such a polarizing topic that disagreement on that aspect alone probably counts for more than its importance in the overall argumentation.

I see AI polarization as a major communication obstacle these days. I have seen professional research scientist refuse to talk to one another because of opposite views on AI. I have _never_ seen something similar on any other form of disagreement.
